# Detecting Fake Review Buyers Using Network Structure: Direct Evidence from Amazon
Sherry He (UCLA Anderson), Brett Hollenbeck (UCLA Anderson), Gijs Overgoor (RIT Saunders), Davide Proserpio (USC Marshall), Ali Tosyali (RIT Saunders)
2022

## What is this paper about?

This paper proposes a network-based approach to detecting products that buy fake reviews on Amazon.com. The central contribution is a unique dataset with ground-truth labels — the authors directly observed which Amazon products purchased fake reviews through private Facebook groups — which allows them to overcome a longstanding limitation in the fake review detection literature: the lack of verified fake reviews with which to train and validate detection models. The paper shows that products buying fake reviews are highly clustered in the product-reviewer network, because fake review buyers must rely on a small shared pool of fake reviewers. Features derived from this network structure (clustering coefficient and eigenvector centrality) outperform text, metadata, and image features at detecting fake review buyers, and critically, are harder for sellers to evade than text-based methods. The approach works even without ground-truth data: unsupervised clustering using network features identifies two clusters containing 70% of predicted fake review buyers while representing only 3.4% of all products.

## Important context

- The dataset is the same as in the companion paper "The Market for Fake Reviews" (MS 2022) — the fake review buyer labels come from monitoring private Facebook groups where sellers purchase reviews.
- The paper distinguishes between detecting fake *reviews* (prior literature) and detecting fake review *buyers* (products). This distinction is important: detecting which products manipulate ratings is more actionable for platforms, and the product-level network approach is not feasible at the individual review level.
- The network-based approach is argued to be more manipulation-resistant than text or metadata features: network features arise from the structural reality of the fake review marketplace (many products sharing a small pool of reviewers), which is very costly to change. Text features, by contrast, can be adapted by reviewers.
- The Home & Kitchen dataset for the unsupervised analysis uses the McAuley et al. Amazon review dataset (~65K products, 11M reviews) — a large publicly available corpus.

## Data and methods

- **Ground-truth dataset:** ~1,500 Amazon products directly observed buying fake reviews via Facebook groups (Oct 2019 – Jun 2020) plus ~2,714 competitor products (same setting as "The Market for Fake Reviews"). Labels are reliable because they come from direct observation of purchases, not inferred from review content.
- **Product-reviewer network:** Bipartite graph where products are nodes and edges exist between two products if they share a reviewer. Network features: degree (number of shared reviewers), eigenvector centrality, PageRank, and clustering coefficient.
- **Additional features:** Metadata (number of reviews, average rating, review timing, 1-star and 5-star ratios, helpful vote ratio, review length variance, TF-IDF text similarity), image features (cosine similarity of product and review images via pre-trained CNN), and text features (TF-IDF vectors, top 1,000 features per product).
- **Supervised approach:** Random forest classifiers trained on 80% of products, evaluated on remaining 20%. Metrics: AUC, accuracy, true positive rate (TPR), true negative rate (TNR), F1.
- **Unsupervised approach:** Applied to full Home & Kitchen Amazon dataset (~65K products). K-means clustering (k=20) using all features; pre-trained classifier estimates proportion of fake review buyers per cluster.

## Key results

1. **Network features dramatically outperform all other approaches:** Among all feature types (network, metadata, text, images), models using only network features achieve the highest AUC, accuracy, TPR, and F1. The two most important individual features are clustering coefficient and eigenvector centrality. A sparse model using just these two features outperforms models trained on all metadata, text, or image features.

2. **Fake review buyers are highly clustered in the network:** Products buying fake reviews share far more reviewers in common than typical products, creating a distinctive clustering signature. This pattern arises because the pool of fake reviewers on Facebook is small relative to the total population of Amazon reviewers.

3. **Unsupervised detection is highly effective:** K-means clustering of the Home & Kitchen dataset using network features identifies two clusters (3.4% of all products) containing approximately 70% of predicted fake review buyers. One of these clusters has an estimated 83% prevalence of fake review buyers — highly distinctive.

4. **Network features are manipulation-resistant:** Text and metadata can be directly modified to evade detection. Network features, by contrast, reflect the structural constraints of the fake review marketplace — sharing reviewers is inherent to how fake reviews are purchased — making them costly to eliminate even by sophisticated actors.

5. **Platform implications:** The paper argues Amazon should target *sellers* (not individual reviewers) and should reduce their search visibility (rather than banning them outright) to offset the financial incentive to buy fake reviews while limiting the risk of false-positive harm.

## Limitations and scope

- **Amazon-specific and time-specific:** The fake review market operates through Facebook groups in a specific period (2019–2020). 
- **No text-based features in network model:** The strongest model uses only two network features. There may be complementarity between network and text features that a more sophisticated model could exploit.
- **Reviewer-level data limitations:** The analysis is product-level; reviewer-level features (e.g., reviewer behavior patterns) are not fully exploited.

## Navigation guide

- **Read first:** Abstract/significance statement and Introduction (the main.tex contains the full paper — not modular). The core argument is: fake review buyers share reviewers → clusters in network → detectable via clustering coefficient and eigenvector centrality.
- **For data:** Data section (Section 1 of main.tex) — ground-truth data collection, Amazon product data, competitor set.
- **For network construction and features:** Subsection "Network construction and network features generation" — graph construction, degree, centrality, PageRank, clustering coefficient.
- **For supervised results:** Subsection "Supervised approach" and Table 2 — random forest classifier performance by feature set.
- **For unsupervised results:** Subsection "Unsupervised approach" and Figure 3 — cluster analysis of Home & Kitchen data.
- **For SI appendix:** SI-Appendix.tex — mathematical details of network measures, classifier technical details, feature correlations, cluster summary statistics.

## Publication status

Published: *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.* 2022, Vol. 119(47).
https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2211932119
https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4147920
https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2211932119
